advantages: Smooth and differentiable everywhere, including x = 0, which avoids the ReLU kink and can stabilize gradients in deep Transformer stacks.
Softly suppresses negative inputs instead of hard-zeroing them, preserving weak signal paths that can help with residual learning.
Strong empirical track record in NLP Transformers (BERT, GPT-2) and many downstream fine-tuning recipes built on those checkpoints.
Probabilistic interpretation ties activation to stochastic regularization, offering a principled alternative to hard gating.
disadvantages: More expensive than ReLU because it evaluates Φ(x) or a tanh/sigmoid approximation on every element.
Exact vs approximate implementations differ slightly; mixing them across training and export can cause small numerical drift.
Not universally better than ReLU—gains are architecture- and task-dependent, and CNNs often still default to ReLU.
Less interpretable than piecewise-linear ReLU; debugging activation saturation requires understanding the Gaussian CDF shape.
